descriptions: active verbs. For example 'he ran to the store in the rain'
static verbs. For example 'He is wet'
multiple verbs about one subject. For example: 'The telegram was late but contained exciting news.' (was, contained)
definition: A predicate is the grammatical term for the words in a sentence or clause that describe the action but not the subject. In many English sentences, the subject names an agent, the doer or source of what is done; the verb names the action, what is done or happens; and the object, if there is one, can name the recipient or goal of the action. When this is the case, the predication is said to be active. Other sentences, such as with linking verbs like ‘is’, cannot be described with agent/action structure. They express states of being and so allow the defining, categorizing, and qualifying that are indispensable in claims of knowledge and principle. This type of predication is referred to as stative.

descriptions: single individuals, proper names, groups or collections of people. Examples: 'Irene', 'a truck driver', 'voters'
where the subject is the writer or reader themsleves. Examples: 'I', 'you', 'we'
anything that has a physical existence. Examples: 'The car swerved', 'the computer crashed'
name referents that cannot be pointed to easily. Examples: 'international trade', 'voting trends', 'athletic ability'
vague, general all-encompassing terms. Examples: 'fairness', 'justice', 'oppresion'
dummy subjects used to make complete sentences. Examples: 'it' in 'it was raining'
often used in headlines for immediacy effect or to emphasize an action. Examples: 'Guerrilas take Afgan capital', 'I call upon the minister to resign!'
Expressing smething that already happened. Example: 'He told reporters'
Expressing something that has yet to happen. Example: 'He will meet with her tomorrow.'
changing tense in the middle of a sentence for effect. Example: 'She was an extraordinary woman, ... but there is no need to canonize your memory. You stand tall.'
Expressing a simple fact. Examples: 'Rover eats bones.', 'Rover ate a bone.'
Expressing a completed action. Exapmle: 'Rover has eaten a bone.'
Expressing an ongoing, habitual, or incomplete action. Example: 'Rover is eating a bone.'
Expressing the end of an ongoing action. Example: 'Rover has been eating a bone.'
denoting a mood of verbs expressing simple statement of a fact.
relating to or denoting a mood of verbs expressing what is imagined or hypothetical or possible.
expressing surprise, strong emotion, or pain.
questions
commands or directives
expressing wishes
the contradiction or denial of something.
language of possibilities with modifiers like 'might', 'likely', 'perhaps'

the practice of turning verbs into nouns (polyptoton). Example: given the sentence 'Irene Curie recieved the Nobel Prize.', the nominalized version would be 'Irene Curie was the recipient of the Nobel Prize.'
endowing physical objects and abstractions with human-like agency. Example: 'The nation weeps for her lost sons.'
multiplying the subjects or verbs in a single predication. Example: 'We shall pay any price, bear any burden, meet any hardship, oppose any foe to assure the survival of liberty.'

clauses that suggest a causal connection with subordinating conjunctions like: 'after', 'when', 'because', 'since', 'although', 'unless', 'while', 'until', 'whereas', 'if', 'as', 'just as'. Example: 'As Richard Neustadt said, political elites are constantly evaluating the president.'
clauses that begin with 'if'. Example: 'If we don't call out hate speach, we perpetuate it.'
clauses using conjunctions like: 'as', 'than'. Example: 'Some of these are as close to Saigon as Balimore is to Washington.'
relative clauses using 'who', 'whom', 'that', 'which'. Examples: 'If a free society cannot save the many who are poor, it cannot save the few who are rich.', 'That man who just came in is drunk.'
clauses often used for indirect speech or paraphrasing or incorporating detail. Example: 'The president said that he would consider the matter.'

Being something, having done something. Choosing to express an action as a participle rather than a verb changes the relation of that action to an agent. A participle phrase is an adjective phrase headed by a participle. Example: 'Waving a flag, John blows the whistle', 'Having boiled the water, I made tea.'

To do something. The adverbial inifinitive can direct the interpretation of other actions in a sentence. Most infinitive phrases that function as adverbs tell us why the action occurred. Example: 'To send a letter, requires a stamp.'
built on a noun, renaming the noun it modifies. Example: 'Botswana, a landlocked nation'
built on a noun, realted to the noun it modifies as a part or feature. Example: 'The driver, his hair invisible under his cap.'
built by repeating the noun it modifies. Example: 'The letter from the president, a letter without precedent.'
built on a noun summarizing or generalizing  preceding clause or phrase. Example: 'The committe voted 10 to 1, a departure from its usual practices.'
Mdify the subject, verb, or adjective
adjectives and adverbs
using multiple modification words and phrases within a single sentence

A sentence that opens with modifying structures and so postpones its subject is said to branch to the left. Example: 'In the distance, above the noise of the crowd, I thought I could hear the sound of guns.'
A sentence that inserts material between the subject and verb. Example: 'Even the children, brought there with the rest of the family, stared intently.'

A sentence that is complete gramatically but continues with modifying clauses or phrases. Example: 'There were hundreds of small groups, each concentrated around someone who was haranguing the others, using the familiar, heavy public gestures of the Chinese.'
A sentence that completes itself gramatically and/or delivers its meaning, often dramatically, at the end. Often, a 'pause' is inserted before the final predication, such as a dash
A sentence that trails off in accumulated detail with no concluding point.
The beginning and end of a sentence recieve the most emphasis. By placing the salient elements in those positions, the writer can influence what is emphasized.
subjects of a sentence recive the most attention
anastrophe and hyperbaton. when the normal order of Subject Verb Object (SVO) is violated. Examples: 'The cedars tall.', 'The man, crooked and homely.'
When a combination of two or more of the above emphasis strategies are used in one sentence.

adjacent phrases or clauses have equal syllable length. Helps consecutive statements cohere as a set. Example: 'They stand against us because we stand in their way.', 'The nation is sick. Trouble is in the land. Confusion all around.'
alteranting stressed and unstressed syllables. Example: 'We do not want to fight. But we have fought before'
parity among longer clauses from repetition. Example: 'That's the kind of soldiers they are That's the kind of people they are'
Example: 'Yesterday climate change was a theory. Today climate change is the reality.'
repetition of clauses or phrases within a passage. Example: 'Our policy is based on evidence; Our policy is rooted in our moral values; Our policy is reinforced by action.'
argument from opposites. Example: 'Buy low, sell high'
argument from inversion. Transposing the arguments in a phrase. Example: 'Ask not what your country can do for you. Ask what you can do for your country.'
sets forth the full meaning and character of a thing lucidly and succinctly. Examples: 'What is virtue? virtue is a responsibility', 'A monarchy is a government headed by a hereditary ruler.'
Conclusion from the specific to the general (genralization)
Generates a conclusion from a set of examples (case based reasoning). Also referred to as 'abduction'.
when items are listed together they are implictly lumped into the same category. Example: 'The all male Saudi rulers, Catholic priesthood, Taliban, and the boys club running Enron.'
Item are listed in an increasing or decreasing order. Example: 'No corporal, sergeant, lietenant, captain, major, or general could order me to do something that was against my religion.'
lists of items with overlapping, suggesting a chain of causality. Example: 'Add to your faith virtue, to virtue knowledge, to knowledge self-control, ....'
using conjunctions between every item. Example: 'The forces of truth and love and courage and honesty.'
not using any conjunctions when listing items. Example: 'I came, I saw, I conquered.'
